title: Divide-and-conquer strategy

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Divide-and-conquer strategy; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Divide-and-conquer strategy
sources.claim: Divide-and-conquer separates a problem into independent subproblems, solves them recursively, and combines their results.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Divide-and-conquer separates a problem into independent subproblems, solves them recursively, and combines their results.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Merge sort divides into halves and linearly combines sorted results. Your goal is to design and analyze a divide-and-conquer solution beyond a memorized sorting example. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Readiness, algorithms and analysis; Array representation and operations; Recurrence reasoning. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Divide-and-conquer separates a problem into independent subproblems, solves them recursively, and combines their results. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: C and C++ expose finite native call stacks without guaranteed tail-call elimination; Java likewise has bounded stack frames; Python has a comparatively low recursion limit, so all four implementations need an iterative alternative when depth scales with input.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Overlapping subproblems favor dynamic programming, unbalanced division harms depth, and combine cost can dominate. The draft therefore rejects: Any algorithm containing recursion is divide-and-conquer.

includes: Divide independent subproblems, solve recursively and combine with controlled duplication.
Design and analyze a divide-and-conquer solution beyond a memorized sorting example.

C and C++ expose finite native call stacks without guaranteed tail-call elimination; Java likewise has bounded stack frames; Python has a comparatively low recursion limit, so all four implementations need an iterative alternative when depth scales with input.
excludes: Overlapping subproblems favor dynamic programming, unbalanced division harms depth, and combine cost can dominate.

outcomes: Design and analyze a divide-and-conquer solution beyond a memorized sorting example.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Divide-and-conquer requires a meaningful division into subproblems plus a combination step, not recursion alone.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: C and C++ expose finite native call stacks without guaranteed tail-call elimination; Java likewise has bounded stack frames; Python has a comparatively low recursion limit, so all four implementations need an iterative alternative when depth scales with input.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Overlapping subproblems favor dynamic programming, unbalanced division harms depth, and combine cost can dominate.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any algorithm containing recursion is divide-and-conquer.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Any algorithm containing recursion is divide-and-conquer.
